summary: McKenzie documents the early development process for Appointment Reminder, his SaaS product for automating appointment reminders via phone, text, and email. After six months of procrastination following the MVP, he set an arbitrary end-of-November deadline and broke the work into half-day mini-quests to maintain momentum. He walks through his technology choices (Rails, jQuery, SASS/HAML), his ruthless feature scoping process where he cut anything not needed on launch day, and his incremental coding approach of getting visible progress quickly. The post serves as a real-time case study in how a solo developer ships a product by cutting scope aggressively and maintaining forward momentum.
key_points: Deadlines with small, measurable subgoals are more effective than open-ended timelines — breaking work into half-day quests creates consistent forward progress
Ruthless scope cutting is essential: features that won't be needed on launch day are out of scope, regardless of how useful they might eventually be
Starting with incremental visual progress (getting index views working, then show, then edit) creates momentum and clear pickup points for the next session
Technology choices should balance productivity with professional growth — Rails for speed, jQuery and SASS as deliberate learning investments
Shipping is only 10% of turning an application into a business; marketing, customer development, and organic SEO are the remaining 90%
money_quotes: While my life is firing on all cylinders, happiness does not write jQuery or Rails code.
Deadlines tend to coerce me into doing my best work. Specifically, deadlines with subgoals that have small units of measurable accomplishment work best for me.

Users need to be able to manage clients. OK. Hypothetically, they may have hundreds or thousands of them, so they'll need a search interface… but will they have hundreds or thousands on launch day? No? Search is out of scope. Next!
Uploading files is a pain in the keister — out of scope!
I am literally an order of magnitude more productive in Rails than I am with Java, and I lack the experience with Java architecture astronomy to build an application from the ground up.
Admin interface: God gave us console for a reason.
After shipping, of course, comes the 90% of the remaining work needed to turn an application into a business.
key_insight: Shipping a product as a solo developer requires ruthless scope cutting — if a feature won't be needed on launch day, it's out of scope, and momentum comes from breaking work into small units of visible, measurable progress.
